title: Disabled Veterans Housing Support Act
plain_summary: This bill helps disabled veterans get housing assistance more easily. When veterans apply for community development housing programs, the government will no longer count their disability payments as income. This means more disabled veterans will qualify for help with housing. The bill also requires the government to study other housing programs to see if they should be changed to help veterans in the same way.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Disabled Veterans Housing Support Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Service Connected Disability Compensation
sections.text: This section changes an existing housing law from nineteen seventy four. It adds a new rule about how to count income when deciding if someone qualifies for housing help. The new rule says that states, local governments, and Indian tribes must not count disability payments from the Department of Veterans Affairs when they figure out if a veteran has low income, moderate income, or low to moderate income. These disability payments are money that veterans get because they were injured or became sick due to their military service. By not counting this money as income, more disabled veterans will qualify for housing assistance programs.

sections.title: Section 3  Report
sections.text: The Comptroller General of the United States must write a report to Congress within one year after this law is passed. The Comptroller General is the head of an office that reviews how the government spends money and runs programs. The report must do three things. First, it must look at how veteran disability payments are handled when deciding who can get help from all programs run by the Secretary of Housing and Urban Development. Second, it must find any cases where veteran disability payments are treated differently than what this new law requires. Third, for each housing program that treats veteran disability payments the wrong way, the report must suggest new laws that would help these programs better serve veterans and communities that do not get enough help.
